People
List alphabetically List by Role
Summaries Details
Roger Brent President and Research Director
510-981-8744brent@molsci.org
Roger was born in Spartanburg, South Carolina in 1955. He received a BA in Computer Science and Mathematics from the University of Southern Mississippi in 1973, where he did some work attempting to apply AI techniques to protein folding. He went on to get a Ph.D. in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology from Harvard University in 1982 for studies with Mark Ptashne. As a graduate student, he showed that the E. coli lexA gene repressed genes involved in the response to radiation damage, cloned the gene, produced and purified its protein product using and in some cases extending the newly developed recombinant DNA methods, and studied binding of the repressor to its operators, showing that its differential binding affinity for these sites affected the timing of the response. As a postdoctoral fellow, also with Mark Ptashne, he tested a number of ideas about the mechanism of transcription regulation in yeast by using the prokaryotic LexA protein and in subsequent experiments creating chimeric proteins that carried LexA fused to activators native to yeast. These "domain swap" experiments established the modular nature of eukaryotic transcription regulators.

In 1985, Roger became a Professor at Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School Department of Genetics. He and his coworkers used yeast transcription that depended on chimeric DNA bound proteins as a genetic probe for protein function in higher organisms. This work led to the development of working two-hybrid methods (1988-1993), to the ability to scale them up via interaction mating (1992-1994), and to the eventual development of protein interaction methods as a useful way to learn more about biological function. In parallel, Roger and his coworkers developed peptide aptamers as reverse "genetic" agents to study the function of proteins and allelic protein variants (1999-2001), and, more recently, as dominant forward "genetic" reagents to identify genes and pathway linkages in organisms, such as human cells, that are intractable to classical genetic analysis. (Perhaps as important as the actual technologies is the coeval development of ideology (e.g. doctrine) for using them.) This work is described in about 80 research papers and reviews.

In parallel to his academic work, Roger is a longtime (since 1984) advisor to the biotech and pharmaceutical industries. He served on the SAB of American Home Products (Genetics Institute/Wyeth Ayerst Research), chairs scientific advisory boards for several smaller companies, and does significant ad hoc consulting work in genomics and computational biology. He is one of the founders (1987-2001) of Current Protocols, including Current Protocols in Molecular Biology, a "how to clone it" manual, which is updated every three months and has about 10,000 subscribing labs. He is founder and organizer (since 1994) of the "After the Genome" workshops. He is an inventor on 11 issued and several pending US Patents. Since the middle 1990s, he has exhorted and advised various bodies in the US and abroad on functional genomics and computational biology, including the National Institutes of Health, the Wellcome Trust, the National Science Foundation, Department of Energy, Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, and other parts of the US Defense Department.

Roger joined the Molecular Sciences Institute in 1998 as Associate Director. He was named Director in 2000 and President and CEO in 2001. Brent joined the faculty of UCSF Department of Biopharmaceutical Sciences as an Adjunct Professor (2000) and was named a Senior Scholar of the Ellison Medical Foundation (2001).
Ian Burbulis Biochemistry and proteomics
510-981-8752harley@molsci.org
BS in Biology, minor in biochemistry (Summa cum laude). Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, VA. 1993
Ph.D. in Molecular and Cellular Biology. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, VA. 1998
Ph.D. thesis title, "Macromolecular organization of flavonoid biosynthesis in Arabidopsis thaliana"
Joined the Molecular Sciences Institute in 1999

Employment:
Independent ceramic tile and marble contractor. Dynamic Tile Co. 6220 Haskin Ct., Burke VA, 22015. (6/87-11/94).
Plant Molecular Biology Research Technician. B. Winkle research group, Virginia Tech. Dept. of Biology, Blacksburg VA. (1/94-12/94).
Research Fellow; Molecular Sciences Institute. 2168 Shattuck Ave. Berkeley, CA. 94704. (3/99-present)

Invited Lectures:
"Analysis of the Arabidopsis thaliana 2YY6 chalcone synthase allele". Plant Molecular Biology Group. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 12/8/94.
"Analysis of an Arabidopsis chalcone synthase mutation: evidence for flavonoid-independent fertility". Sixth International conference on Arabidopsis research. Madison, WI. 6/95-11/95.
"Flavonoid-independent fertility in Arabidopsis". Botany seminar series. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VS. 9/8/95.
"Assembly of malate dehydrogenase and citrate synthase into a model enzyme complex". Plant Molecular Biology Group. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 3/3/96.
"A historical and contemporary perspective of bacteriophage genetics" BIOL: 4634 (Microbial Genetics, 3 credits). Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 4/3/96.
"Molecular tools used to dissect cellular mechanisms". BIOL: 3774 (Molecular Biology, 3 credits). Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 3/26/97.
"Molecular mechanisms of neurons and neural networks". BIOL:3124 (Cell Physiology, 3 credits). Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 4/28/97.
"Analysis of flavonoid metabolism in Arabidopsis". Seventy-fifth Annual meeting of the Virginia Academy of Sciences. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 5/23/97.
"Recombinant-DNA technology" BIOL: 3774 (Molecular Biology, 3 credits). Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 2/20/98.

Grants:
"Analysis of multienzyme catalytic domains involved with substrate product channeling." Funded by Sigma Xi Grants in Aid of Research $1,000.00. 1/20/94.
Travel Fund award. $300.00 Graduate Student Association. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 1/5/95.
Graduate Student Travel Fund award. $205.00 For travel to the Gordon Conference: Macromolecular Structure and Cell Function, Oxford, England.
Graduate Student Association. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 10/5/96. "Thermodynamic analysis of flavonoid enzyme complex assembly". $1,000.00.
Graduate Research Development Program. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg Va. 12/27/96.
"Thermodynamic analysis of the flavonoid enzyme complex" $1,000.00. Funded by Sigma Xi Grants-in-Aid-of-Research. 1/21/97.
Graduate Student Travel Fund award. $243.00 awarded for travel to the Gordon Conference: Biological Regulatory Mechanisms. Graduate Student Association. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg VA. 7/10/98.

Membership:
Phi Beta Kappa, 5/93. Mu chapter, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, VA.

Honors:
Academic dean's list: 5/91, 12/93.
Distinguished University honors: 12/90, 12/91, 5/92, 12/92, 5/93.
Fairfax County Medical Society Scholarship: $1,500.00; Awarded on a competitive basis. 7/91, 7/92, and 7/94.
"Best Student Paper/Presentation" 75th Annual Meeting of the Virginia Academy of Sciences. Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, VA. 5/23/97.

Inventions:
"A method for spectroscopic protein indentification" Robert Carlson, Ian Burbulis, Roger Brent. Patent pending.
"Sensitive detection of ligand binding and protein conformation using metal nanoparticles" Robert Carlson, Ian Burbulis. Patent pending.
"High-throughput selection of protein-aptamers" Ian Burbulis. Patent pending.

Current research projects include developing methods to identify and quantify tens of proteins from complex mixtures simultaneously, creating new kinds of functional interaction maps in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, and understanding how molecular organization of kinases potentiate signaling pathways in living cells.
Charles Cantor Board of Trustees
Sequenom, Inc. and BUlha@molsci.org
Charles R. Cantor, Ph.D. is Chairman of Sequenom, Inc.'s Scientific Advisory Board and was appointed Chief Scientific Officer in June 1998. Sequenom is a biotechnology company in San Diego, California that is developing technology to analyze single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs). SNPs are the most common form of genetic variation and are expected to play an essential role in the future of drug development, diagnostics and other life science applications. These variations represent the origin of most differences between individuals, including predisposition to disease, drug tolerance and drug efficacy. Dr. Cantor was previously the chair and professor of the department of biomedical engineering and biophysics, and director of the Center for Advanced Biotechnology at Boston University. Prior to this Dr. Cantor held positions at Columbia University. He was also director of the Human Genome Center Project of the Department of Energy at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory. Dr. Cantor is a consultant to more than 16 biotech firms, has published more than 325 peer reviewed articles, been granted 26 US patents, and co-authored a three-volume textbook on Biophysical Chemistry. He recently completed the first textbook on Genomics: The Science and Technology of the Human Genome Project.
Rob Carlson Applied Minds, Inc.
rcarlson@molsci.org
B.S. University of Washington, 1992
Ph.D. Physics, Princeton 1997
Ph.D. thesis title, "Deformation activation of human leukocytes"

Research Fellow, Molecular Sciences Institute (1997-2002)
Research Fellow emeritus, Molecular Sciences Institute, 2002
Joined Applied Minds in 2002

Interested in discovering what kinds of problems single cells can solve and how they interact with their environment. Also interested in the related question of how information flows from the environment into the genome. Currently working on techniques to measure internal states of cells, such as quantifying the expressed protein complement, and the related problem of quantifying protein-protein interactions. Previous experience includes; single neuron recording in a fly, experiment and modeling of human leukocyte behavior after physiological deformation, and developing new microfabrication techniques for use in biology, optics, fluorescence microscopy, and spectroscopy.
Denise Caruso Board of Trustees
The Hybrid Vigor Institutelha@molsci.org
Denise Caruso is founder and executive director of the Hybrid Vigor Institute in San Francisco, California, which seeks to cultivate the conduct of rigorous interdisciplinary research and to facilitate a new approach to knowledge production based on disciplinary diversity. Also a veteran journalist and technology analyst, Caruso has for nearly 20 years chronicled the converging industries of digital technology, telecommunications and interactive media. From October 1995 until April 2000, she wrote the Technology column for the Monday business section of The New York Times. In January 2000, Ms. Caruso became an occasional contributor to The New York Times' Arts & Ideas section, writing primarily about scientific and academic research in progress. She was one of the earliest advocates of First Amendment rights in cyberspace, and one of the first journalists to focus on technology, commerce and culture. She has served on the board of directors of the Electronic Frontier Foundation, and serves as an advisor to the Center for Public Knowledge.
Tina Chin Experimental methods development
510-981-8721tina@molsci.org
Education:

B. S. Microbiology, 1992, University of California, Davis

Job Positions:

Senior Research Technician/ Lab Manager July 1998 to present The Molecular Sciences Institute Berkeley, CA

Assistant Specialist II June 1992 to June 1998 Immunochemistry Facility, College of Natural Resources University of California, Berkeley, CA

Patents and Licensing Disclosures:

Stereospecific anti-imidazolinone antibodies, Karu, A. E., Chin, T. E., Pont, J., Wong, R. B., patent applications filed in USA, Argentina, Brazil and Europe.

1994 Karu, A. E., T. E. Chin and C. W. Bell. Recombinant antibodies specific for imidazolinone herbicides. Licensing Disclosure, University of California, Berkeley (Dec. 1994).

1993 Karu, A. E., R. Wong, J. Pont and T. E. Chin. Monoclonal antibodies, haptens, and immunoassays for detection of imidazolinone herbicides. Licensing Disclosure, University of California, Berkeley (Oct. 1993).

Research Interests:

1998 to present-- Protein-protein interactions in yeast

- Detect interactions of structurally conserved regions of a protein via two-hybrid system in yeast.

- Made an improved prey vector for two-hybrid system in order to clone by homologous recombination.

- Created numerous S. cerevisiae gene deletions and clonings for study of the gene, CBK1.

1997 to 1998-- Antibody engineering and structure

- Identification of anti-imidazolinone scFv combining site through computer homology modeling using a database of antibody crystallographic structures in collaboration with Dr. Victoria Roberts, Scripps Research Institute, La Jolla, CA.

- Validation of anti-imidazolinone antibody model by in vitro site-directed mutagenesis.

- Conversion of scFv to disulfide-stabilized Fv (dsFv) by mutagenesis.

Responsibilities include:

- Lab Safety and Radiation Compliance Officer

- Institute Lab Manager

- Supervisor of one research technician and one lab assistant

Alejandro Colman-Lerner Experimental and computational biology
510-981-8716alerner@molsci.org
Education
B.A., Biology, University of Buenos Aires, 1992
Ph.D. Biology, University of Buenos Aires, 1997
Ph.D. thesis title "Regulation of Ovarian Follicular Growth and Maturation by Growth Factors and Extracellular Matrix Components"

Professional Experience
1998 - present Research Fellow, Molecular Sciences Institute

Current Research

Co-investigator of the Alpha Project. Current research projects include studies of cell polarity establishment and signal transduction of S.cerevisiae, experiments to support computational modeling of the mating pheromone response in S.cerevisiae, use of aptamer libraries to probe complex mammalian phenotypes and to create an array of affinity reagents to study the S.cerevisiae proteome.

Drew Endy Modeling, methods development, experimental integration (MIT)
617 258 5152endy@mit.edu
B.S. Civil Engineering, Lehigh University, 1992
M.S. Environmental Engineering, Lehigh University, 1994
Ph.D. Biochemical Engineering, Dartmouth College, 1998
Ph.D. thesis title "Development and Application of a Genetically-Structured Simulation for Bacteriophage T7"

Joint postdoctoral appointment at University of Texas at Austin Microbiology and University of Wisconsin at Madison Chemical Engineering (1998)
Research Fellow, Molecular Sciences Institute, (1998 - 2001)
MIT Department of Biology and Division of Biological Engineering, (2002-present)

Current research projects include the de novo synthesis of designed biological systems, a computational/ experimental study of the yeast pheromone signal transduction pathway, and the development of new computational methods for representing large systems of biochemical reactions.
Andrew Gordon Modeling and methods development
510-981-8751agordon@molsci.org
Education:
A.B. Physics, Princeton University, 1990
M.S. Electrical Engineering, Columbia Unversity, 1991
Ph.D. Experimental High Energy Particle Physics Harvard University, 1998
Ph.D. thesis title: "A Measurement of the W Boson Mass with the Collider Detector at Fermilab"

Professional Experience:
Post-doctoral training at NYU Medical Center working on low light level detection of fluorescent molecules (1999-2000)
Lucent Technologies writing monte carlo calculations of wireless network server capacities (2000-2001)
Joined the Molecular Sciences Institute in 2001

Current Research:

Current research interests include low-signal fluorescence microscopy as well as computational modeling of the alpha factor signaling pathway in S.cerevisiae. The focus of the computational work is to produce a model with parameters that can be directly constrained by experiments being done at the Institute and also to use the data to determine and constrain the physical parameters of the model.
Jeremy Gordon
jgordon@molsci.org
Lauren Ha Administration
510-981-8742lha@molsci.org
Lauren was born in Saigon, Vietnam in 1970 and came to the U.S. in 1975. She received a B.S. in Biology from the University of South Carolina in 1992 and has been active in biology related volunteer work since the 1980s. After college, she began her lab administration career in a technology-intensive biological research lab in the Department of Molecular Biology at Massachusetts General Hospital. She joined the Molecular Sciences Institute in January 1998 and was named Vice-President of Administration in July 1998.

Lauren is responsible for the operations of the MSI including human resources, finance and accounting, administration, grants management, public relations, and facilities. She has also been a management consultant for other research organizations since 1999. She is also a member of the steering committee of the MSI Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation. Lauren is devoted to the continued development of the MSI's unique internal scientific environment and is helping to lead funding and strategic planning efforts.
Janice Hamer Experimental methods development
510-981-8701jhamer@molsci.org
Laboratory Technician
Thomas Kalil Board of Trustees
UC Berkeleylha@molsci.org
Thomas Kalil is one of former President Clinton?s key technology advisors, becoming deputy assistant to the President for technology and economic policy and deputy director of the National Economic Council. He advised President Clinton on a wide range of science, technology and telecommunications issues, including the National Nanotechnology Initiative, the Next Generation Internet, and efforts to expand funding for the physical sciences and engineering. He was recently named Assistant to the Chancellor of the University of California, Berkeley (2001) and will be working to develop research and education initiatives at the school.

510-981-8745kinney@molsci.org

510-981-8754jkroll@molsci.org

Ph.D., Molecular Biology and Genetics, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine and the Russian Academy of Sciences, 1995
Ph.D. thesis title: "The Role of the CTF18/CHL12 gene in the chromosome cycle of the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae"

Professional Experience:
Postoctoral studies at Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center with Lee Hartwell and Steven Friend (1996-1999)
Joined the Molecular Sciences Institute in 2000

Current Research:

Research interests include chromosome stability in yeast and in cancer, microsatellites and other markers for genomic instability, genomic approaches for functional screening, evolution of control elements of genes, genetic control of eukaryotic adaptation to changing environments and making biological data suitable for computational analysis.
Larry Lok Biological modeling; simulation methods development
510-981-8740lok@molsci.org
Education:
BA, Mathematics, University of Southern Mississippi, 1976
M.A. Mathematics, Columbia University, 1979
Ph.D. Mathematics, Columbia University, 1984
Ph.D. thesis title "Deformations of Locally Homogenous Spaces and Kleinian Groups"

Professional Experience:
Postdoctotal faculty experience at Cornell University and the University of Southern Mississippi
9 years post PhD industrial experience at Intergraph, Inc., and Veribest, Inc, writing design and digital simulation software

Current Research:

Current research projects include using stochastic simulation techniques to extrapolate complex biochemical reaction networks and accelerating stochastic simulation of reaction networks. Other recent research projects have included inference of Markov chain models to study gene regulation and a graph-based search tool for a database of protein-protein interactions.

Andy Mendelsohn Cell cycle and apoptosis

Education:
B.A. University of Pennsylvania in Biophysics and Natural Science in 1981
Ph.D. in Molecular and Cellular Biology at Brown University in 1991 studying how master genes control myogenesis and cell proliferation.
Ph.D. thesis title "Inhibition of myogenesis in vitro, characterization of effects of oncogene transformation."

Professional Experience:
Postdoctoral work investigating cellular logic and control of the cell cycle and apoptosis at Massachusetts General Hospital Department of Molecular Biology with Roger Brent (1991-1998)
Joined the Institute in 1998

Current Research:

Current research interests are evolutionary mechanisms of cellular maturation and death.
Carolyn Raney Administration
510-647-0690craney@molsci.org
Orna Resnekov Modeling, methods development, mass spectrometry
510-981-8747oresnekov@molsci.org
Education:
B.S. English, 1981
M.S. Biology, 1983, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor.
Ph.D. Virology, 1989, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot, Israel.
Thesis Title: "Transcription Termination in Animal Viruses and Cells".


Postdoctoral studies:
1988-1991 Karolinska Institute, Stockholm, Sweden.
1991-1997 Bunting Institute Science Scholar and Postdoctoral fellow, Harvard University, Cambridge.


Selected Awards & Fellowships:
1989 The Dr. Ester Halinger Prize for Ph.D. studies.
1991-1992 Science Scholar, Bunting Institute of Radcliffe College.
1990-1991 Swedish Cancer Society.
1988-1990 European Molecular Biology Organization Postdoctoral Fellowship.


Professional Experience:
1998-2000 Senior Staff Fellow, NIH-National Institute of Child Health and Human Development, Section on Microbial Genetics.
2000 Joined TMSI, Berkeley, Ca.
2002 Co-investigator, CEGS at TMSI


Current Research:

Co-investigator, the Alpha Project

My main focus is the development of genome-scale population measurement technologies to quantify proteins and their modifications in cells. I am presently concentrating on understanding information flow in yeast cells through the quantification of phosphorylation states of proteins in the Alpha-factor pathway using mass spectrometry. In this work I develop and use technology, analyze results, train researchers and graduate students, coordinate the efforts of my collaborators (Dr. Julie Leary, UC-Berkeley; and Dr. Richard Smith, Pacific National Laboratory) and their coworkers, and make data publicly available on the TMSI Alpha Project website. Results from these approaches are also incorporated into computer simulations of the Alpha factor pathway. In this interdisciplinary work I work closely with Dr. Lok (TMSI), Dr. Endy (MIT), Dr. Bruck (CalTech), Dr. Burbulis (TMSI), Dr. Serra (TMSI), Dr. Yu (TMSI) and Dr. Colman-Lerner (TMSI). In other work, I am developing technologies for the simultaneous analysis of multiple cell surface molecules to discriminate cancer types and to evaluate the physiology of cancer cells. I previously worked on the Gram-positive microorganism, Bacillus subtilis where I acquired extensive experience studying signal transduction using genetics, protein purification and the analysis and use of GFP derivatives. At TMSI, I also champion the inclusion of greater segments of our population in genomic research through two Center-based programs, the Education and Training Plan and the Underrepresented Minority Recruitment, Training and Outreach Plan.
Eduard Serra Mechanisms of cellular self-renewal and differentiation, peptide aptamer development
510-981-8726serra@molsci.org
Education
B.S. in Biology, University of Barcelona, 1995
Ph.D. in Biology, University of Barcelona, 2001

Ph.D. thesis title, "Genetic, cellular and molecular studies of neurofibromas"

Joined the Institute in October 2001

Graduate Research

Previous work studying the role of the NF1 gene in the development of neurofibromas (benign peripheral nervous system tumors) and the characterization of a mutational somatic spectrum for this gene in these heterogeneous tumors. Also involved the demonstration of Schwann cells as the specific cell type that initiate neurofibroma formation and the study of mitotic recombination as a mutational genetic mechanisms in this cell type.

Current Research

His current research interest is the use of peptide aptamers as a tool for performing forward and reverse genetics in mammalian cells. Involved in Designing Improved Peptide Aptamer Libraries, with Ian Burbulis and Richard Yu. In particular, he is interested in applying this technology for studying embryonic stem cells, focussing on genetic pathways that govern self-renewal and specific cell fate differentiation.

He is also collaborating on the Alpha Project.
David Soergel Bioinformatics databases and computation
510-981-8746soergel@molsci.org
B.S. in Symbolic Systems, Stanford University, 1998
Joined the Institute in 2001

Current projects concern bioinformatics databases and computation, supporting research into the alpha factor signaling pathway in S. cerevisiae. Software in development includes a collaborative online tool for building, simulating, visualizing, and analyzing models of complex biochemical systems. Previous work included research in human population genetics, particularly concerning correlations between genes, culture, and geography, as well as various commercial and nonprofit software projects.
Becky Stiewig Administration
510-981-8741bstiewig@molsci.org
Accounting and Finance
Kevin Sweeney Board of Trustees
Shook, Hardy, & Bacon, LLPlha@molsci.org
Kevin Sweeney, J.D. is a Partner in the Corporate Finance and Banking section of Shook, Hardy & Bacon L.L.P., an international law firm headquartered in Kansas City, Missouri. Shook, Hardy & Bacon is one of the 50 largest law firms in the United States, with offices in 10 cities throughout the United States and Europe. Kevin has spent 20 years at the firm leading merger and acquisition transactions, public and private securities offerings, formations of joint ventures and strategic alliances, venture capital investments and other finance and corporate transactions. He has extensive experience in advising top management and Boards of Directors of public and private companies on financial and corporate strategy matters and in coordinating the advice of investment and commercial bankers, accountants, and other experts to help his clients achieve their business and financial goals. He often represents pharmaceutical, biotech, and scientific research entities and a significant number of the matters he handles have cross-border implications.

Kevin received his Bachelor of Business Administration (Management Concentration), cum laude, from the University of Notre Dame in 1979 and his Juris Doctorate, with honors, from the University of Missouri ? Columbia School of Law in 1982. While in law school, he served as the Associate Editor-in-Chief of the Missouri Law Review. He serves as an Adjunct Professor of Law at his alma mater, teaching an advanced course in ?Mergers & Acquisitions.?
Dawn Warfield Assistant to the President
510-981-8743dwarfield@molsci.org
Richard Yu Modeling, single cell measurement methods, peptide aptamer development, structural biology
510-981-8750ryu@molsci.org

Education

  • B.A. Molecular Cell Biology, emphasis in Biophysics, and minor in Computer Science, University of California at Berkeley, 1993
  • Ph.D. Molecular Biophysics and Biochemistry, Yale University, 2000

Professional Experience

  • Research Associate, Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory, Physical Biosciences, 1993-4

Current Research

Research Fellow, TMSI

The development of more predictive models in the Alpha Project depends on accurate measurements of the numbers of relevant protein molecules, protein-protein interaction Kds / on-off rates, and localization, as well as the time-dependent nature of these quantities. I am developing tools to measure these quantities, which fall broadly into two categories. For single cell-resolution measurements, I am developing fluorescent protein-based, in vivo sensors to measure protein interactions and phosphorylation events in real time. For in vitro assays and non-classical forward and reverse genetics, I am working on a couple classes of improved peptide aptamer libraries. Additionally, I am applying more established biochemical and biophysical techniques to quantify specific proteins from small populations and measure their association/dissociation kinetics in vitro. All of this information will be fed into the Alpha factor pathway simulation and improve our understanding of the dynamics of the system.
